briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 23, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Europe Hit by Climate Crisis-Induced Drought and Ecosystem Threats
sections.content: Across Europe, severe drought and soil moisture depletion are causing distress, with scientists pointing out that rising temperatures have exacerbated these droughts by increasing water evaporation by up to 80 times [3, 158, 159, 169, 188]. The impacts of climate change are directly evident in the agricultural sector, where an increase in greenhouse gases has led to taller rice plants but reduced grain yields [2]. Furthermore, the vulnerability of ecosystems is being exposed, as exemplified by the overwintering of bees being dependent on temperature stability [6], and widespread coral bleaching occurring due to human-induced climate change [171, 178]. Global ecosystems and human lives are under threat, with tropical forest trees dying at the fastest rate on record, and millions in small island nations facing deepening food insecurity [175, 180].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Climate Change Adaptation Efforts and Agricultural Sector Innovation
sections.content: Domestically, various adaptation efforts to address climate change are actively underway. The Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries is ensuring the sustainability of the future fisheries industry by mandating the inclusion of climate change impacts and response measures in the Aquaculture Industry Development Plan [1, 35, 152]. In the agricultural sector, customized technologies for climate change response are being disseminated. Jangsu County has confirmed the effectiveness of sun-shading nets in reducing sunscald damage to apples [5, 8, 22, 26], and Dangjin City is also enhancing agricultural resilience by promoting customized agricultural technologies and new fruit tree technologies in response to abnormal weather conditions [18, 20, 23]. The Rural Development Administration has been recognized for its climate crisis adaptation capabilities, selected as a best practice for three consecutive years, and is dedicated to field-specific research and policy development for preventing pests and diseases in response to climate change [14, 21, 25, 29, 34]. The Korea Forest Service is concretizing pine tree management strategies to address climate change and is making comprehensive efforts to protect forest ecosystems by gathering opinions from the public and experts [4, 9, 11, 12, 17].

sections.title: 🌱 Strengthening Policy Foundations and Expanding Citizen Participation for Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: In the National Assembly, an amendment to the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act, reflecting the Constitutional Court's decision, passed a subcommittee, establishing mid-to-long-term reduction targets to be legislated: 53-61% reduction by 2035 and 69-80% reduction by 2040 [51, 82, 86, 94, 102, 107, 110, 112]. Industries are also participating in the transition to carbon neutrality. The display industry has reduced greenhouse gases by 23% over the past five years and successfully developed eco-friendly alternative gases for carbon-neutral processes, offering technological solutions [36, 39, 40, 41, 67]. The textile industry has partnered with the Korea Energy Agency to transition to carbon neutrality and build an energy and greenhouse gas data infrastructure [42, 46, 49, 59, 66, 76, 84]. Local governments and civil society are also actively involved. Siheung City launched a citizen-led 'Climate Citizen Network' to initiate carbon neutrality practices [43, 50, 54, 68, 73, 79, 81, 89, 93, 97, 116], and various campaigns are underway to spread a culture of practical action in daily life, such as Gwangmyeong City's '1.5℃ Climate Volunteers' and Suwon City's 'Carbon Neutral Dream Playground' [38, 44, 71, 85, 92, 96, 108, 115]. Jeonju City continues comprehensive efforts, including providing incentives to households practicing carbon neutrality, thereby encouraging voluntary citizen participation [52, 60, 78, 99].

sections.title: ⚡ Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Facing Challenges
sections.content: Globally, the transition to renewable energy is accelerating. China plans to increase its renewable energy consumption by 53% by 2030 [118, 123], and domestic company Tae Rim Packaging aims to expand renewable energy production, including installing solar power facilities at 80% of its workplaces by 2028 [119, 121, 124, 139, 149, 155]. Amidst a surge in international oil prices, renewable energy-related stocks are showing strength [125, 127, 136, 145, 147, 156]. KEPCO KDN and Yeonggwang County, among others, are expanding public-interest renewable energy projects and promoting the Naju-style 'sunshine pension' model, thereby building resident-participatory models [130, 131, 134, 137, 141, 144, 146, 151, 154]. However, challenges that need to be addressed alongside the expansion of renewable energy supply are also emerging. The phenomenon where carbon emissions do not decrease despite increased renewable energy deployment is being pointed out as a 'grid' issue rather than a 'supply' issue [16, 91], and economic burdens such as concerns about electricity bill increases when using renewable energy are also being discussed [120]. Furthermore, demands for improving public accessibility and efficient infrastructure construction are being raised, including controversies over the method of promoting large-scale renewable energy facilities in Jeju's Gujwa area and issues of private monopolies in solar and wind power facilities in the Jeonnam-Gwangju region [126, 138]. Nevertheless, efforts to expand renewable energy and manage it efficiently will continue, with Gyeonggi Province promoting a demonstration project to utilize residential solar power for RE100 and easing regulations on renewable energy setback distances [132, 143, 148].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the realistic threats of climate change and the multifaceted efforts, both domestic and international, to address them. We have once again confirmed that enhancing adaptability and accelerating the transition towards carbon neutrality are our society's top priorities amidst the escalating climate crisis. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: Climate crisis-driven heat ‘sucking soils dry’ as extreme drought hits Europe
articles.description: Analysis shows how heatwaves are making atmosphere thirstier, damaging water supplies, food production and power generationExtreme heat supercharged by the climate crisis is sucking Europe dry in summer and intensifying the drought hitting the continent, a scientific analysis has found.Record-breaking heatwaves meant the hot atmosphere strongly evaporated water from rivers, lakes, soils and plants – conditions made 80 times more likely by global heating in western Europe and 40 times more likely

summary.phenomenon: The European continent is suffering from severe summer droughts, with record-breaking heatwaves in Western Europe causing strong atmospheric evaporation of moisture. These conditions have become 80 times more likely in Western Europe and 40 times more likely in other regions due to global warming.
summary.cause: Extreme heat, intensified by the climate crisis, is making the atmosphere drier and strongly evaporating moisture from rivers, lakes, soils, and plants, thereby exacerbating drought.
summary.outlook: Drought is causing damage to Europe's water supply, food production, and power generation.
